mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the Dead Sea Scrolls including biblical manuscripts, sectarian documents, and their significance for understanding Second Temple Judaism — a precise match to Qumran literature (B.2).

Directly covers the Talmud including its structure (Mishnah and Gemara), halakha, aggadah, and its role in Jewish legal reasoning and identity — a precise match to Talmud and Midrash (B.3) and Jewish practices and institutions (C.2).
Angels in Judaism including seraphim, cherubim, and archangels from biblical and apocryphal sources — relevant to mystical and devotional writings (B.5) and Jewish myth and legend (B.7).
Biblical typology as an interpretive method reading Old Testament events as prefigurations directly addresses Judaic exegesis and hermeneutics (B.4).
The development of Satan from a minor figure in the Hebrew Bible addresses Jewish myth and legend (B.7) and doctrines concerning God and the supernatural (C.1.a).
Hebrew Sheol and the development of resurrection belief directly addresses Jewish eschatology and views about the future age (C.1.e).
The Hebrew Gehenna as a concept of the afterlife is relevant to Jewish eschatological doctrines (C.1.e).

Moses Mendelssohn's defence of religious toleration, the Haskalah (Jewish Enlightenment), and the compatibility of Judaism with Enlightenment reason directly address developments in modern Judaism (A.5) and Jewish philosophical writings (B.6).
Wyclif's advocacy for vernacular Bible translation and primacy of Scripture raises hermeneutic questions relevant to Judaic biblical interpretation (B.4), though primarily a Christian figure.
